title: Separating a poorly sheep for treatment
subtitle: Getting the affected ewe out of the flock without losing the rest of the morning to it.
excerpt: A sheep standing apart from the flock with her head dropped wants pulling out for a closer look, antibiotics, or just observation away from competition at the trough. Most flocks have no proper hospital pen, so the working answer is a small portable one set up where the work happens. A walk through catching her, moving her, what the pen needs, and when the rest of the flock comes back into the picture.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code for Sheep (statutory UK welfare code). AHDB Beef & Lamb, on-farm flock health planning. SAC Consulting Scotland, separating-and-isolating sheep notes. River Cottage Handbook 14 (Pigs), referenced for the hospital-pen layout that applies cross-species.

glossaryTerms.definition: A narrow corridor (around 70 cm wide for sheep) that funnels animals one-by-one toward a holding gate or a turn-over crate. The single most useful piece of permanent handling kit on a small flock.
Catching a sheep by hooking the curved head of a shepherd's crook over her neck or hind leg. Slower than a race; the only tool when a single sheep needs catching in an open field.
An oral liquid dose (worming, mineral, electrolyte) given over the back of the tongue with a calibrated dosing gun.
An intact male sheep. Tups are housed separately from ewes for most of the year and brought in for autumn mating.

aliases: pulling a sheep out of the flock

recipeTools.notes: Or a clean 25-30 L plastic bucket clipped to the hurdles.
If the separation is for a drench or a wormer dose.
Foot-rot or wound work often wants this on hand.

content.text: A ewe standing on her own at the back of the field with her head dropped and her ears slightly out wants pulling. Sheep are flock animals and a sheep deciding she would rather be alone is almost always telling you something. The trouble might be lameness, mastitis, a worm burden, or just a slow loss of condition under flock pressure at the trough. Each of those wants treating differently; all of them are easier when she is in a small pen on her own with food, water, and the keeper coming twice a day.

Most small flocks do not have a permanent hospital pen. The working answer is four hurdles tied into a square, set up where the work fits the daily round, with the affected sheep in it and the rest of the flock going on with their grazing. The job from spot to settled is usually under an hour.
When to do this

The cue is one of three. A sheep visibly lame on a particular leg through more than a single day. A ewe sitting apart from the flock with her flank hollow on one side after eating, or her udder swollen and hot to the touch (mastitis). A bird-glance during routine handling that flags a low condition score against the rest of the flock. Any of those is worth a closer look in a pen rather than trying to catch and treat in the field.
What you need
Four hurdles (lambing hurdles work well; longer 4 ft hurdles fine too) and four short tie-pieces of orange baler twine.
A bale of clean straw for bedding.
A small hay rack and a water bucket on a clip.
A shepherd's crook.
Whatever the treatment requires: drenching gun, foot spray, iodine, antibiotic if prescribed.
Set the pen up before you catch her

Choose a spot that is dry underfoot, sheltered from prevailing wind, and walkable from the house. A corner of a barn or against a stone wall is ideal; if you are on grass, pick a high spot rather than the field bottom. Tie four hurdles into a square roughly 2 by 2 metres. Spread a thin layer of straw across the floor (thicker in winter, sparing in summer). Hang the hay rack on one side, clip the water bucket on the opposite side so she does not soil it from the rack. Leave the pen ready before you go to fetch the sheep.
Catching her in the field
Two routes. If you can move the whole flock into a corner or up a
race

, you can pick the affected ewe out of the group with a quiet hand under her chin and a foot blocking her path. This is by far the easier route on most holdings. If the flock will not move, or there is no race,
crook handling

is the answer: walk her gently into a corner, set the crook over the back of her neck, and lift it just enough to slow her without choking. Take her to sitting on her rump in the field if she resists; a sheep on her rump is calm and easy to halter for the walk back.
Moving her to the pen

Sheep go where they can see other sheep. Moving one on her own is far harder than moving two. If the pen is more than a short walk from the field, take a companion ewe with her for the journey and put the companion back in the flock once they are at the pen. The affected ewe will follow the companion into the gateway without protest.
If she is too lame to walk well, lift her into a wheelbarrow with the straw bale on top of her chest as a cushion and take her round on wheels. A short ride beats a forced limp on a poorly leg.
Settling her in

Put her in the pen and stand back for ten minutes. She will pace, call to the flock, and then settle. While she does, check the hay rack is loaded, the water is filled to mid-bucket, and any treatment kit is in reach. Then carry out whatever the visit calls for: foot trim and spray, a
drench
, an antibiotic injection per the vet's instruction, or just observation for a sheep whose flank looked hollow.

attrs.title: What good pen care looks like
attrs.body: Twice-daily visits, morning and evening. Fresh hay each visit, fresh water if the bucket has gone dusty or got soiled. A two-minute look at her posture, ears, eyes, and the affected part each time. The bedding gets a fork-over each evening and a top-up of clean straw twice a week. A sheep in a quiet pen often improves visibly inside 48 hours; if she has not, the cause needs revisiting.

content.text: When she goes back

Three checks before returning her to the flock. She is eating hay and drinking water steadily. The condition that brought her in is visibly improving (the lame leg bears weight; the udder has settled; her flank has filled out). She holds her own against a companion ewe brought in to test how she will travel. If those three line up, she goes back at the next quiet moment of the day, and you watch from a distance for ten minutes to see the rest of the flock accept her back in.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Common pen-care outcomes
attrs.intro: What you might see during the separation and what to do about each.

items.symptom: She paces and calls non-stop after the first hour.
items.cause: Flock-bonded ewe, not settling on her own.
items.fix: Move a companion ewe into the pen with her. Pen mates calm each other; the treatment still happens with two.

items.symptom: She has not touched the hay after 24 hours but drinks well.
items.cause: Poor appetite from whatever brought her in; not a feeding problem yet.
items.fix: Offer a small handful of ewe nuts or a slice of damp sugar-beet pulp instead of hay. If she still refuses 36 hours in, contact the vet for a closer look.

items.symptom: The lame leg has not improved in 5 days.
items.cause: More than a footbath job; could be joint-ill, deep infection, or a fracture.
items.fix: Vet visit. A small flock cannot diagnose joint infections from outside; antibiotics may want adjusting.

items.symptom: Bedding is soaked within a day even though she is not lying in it.
items.cause: Either a leak from the water bucket or scour (loose dung).
items.fix: Check the bucket clip first. If it is scour, take a dung sample to the vet for a faecal egg count and pull her off any high-protein feed in the meantime.

items.symptom: She goes back into the flock and gets headed off by other ewes.
items.cause: She has lost rank during the separation; the rest of the flock has reorganised.
items.fix: Pull her back into the pen for 24 hours, then re-introduce alongside the quietest sheep in the flock for a few days. Most rank disputes settle within a week.

content.text: Note on tups
A
tup

in a hospital pen wants a heavier hurdle build and a separate water bucket sized for his neck reach. Tups push at fittings far harder than ewes; the standard light lambing hurdles bend. If a tup needs separating regularly during the year, a permanent timber-and-wire pen against a barn wall is the better answer than an annual rebuild.
